questions.explanation: Yeltsin's June 1991 victory gave him an independent electoral mandate separate from Gorbachev and the Soviet center. That legitimacy became crucial during the August coup, when Yeltsin emerged as the most visible symbol of resistance to the hardliners.

questions.prompt: What did the Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic's June 1990 Declaration of State Sovereignty assert?

choices.text: That Russian laws would take precedence over Soviet union laws on Russian territory
That Russia was immediately withdrawing from the Soviet Union
choices.note: The declaration asserted sovereignty within the USSR, but it was not an immediate declaration of Russian independence.

choices.text: That Gorbachev would simultaneously serve as Russia's president
choices.note: The declaration did not make Gorbachev head of the Russian republic.

choices.text: That private land ownership was fully legalized across the USSR
choices.note: The declaration was about sovereignty and legal authority, not a blanket privatization of land throughout the Soviet Union.

questions.explanation: Russia's sovereignty declaration was a major step in the late Soviet 'war of laws,' in which republican institutions increasingly claimed authority over the union center. When the largest republic asserted the supremacy of its own laws, it further hollowed out the cohesion of the Soviet state.

questions.prompt: What was the main political innovation of the Soviet Union's 1989 elections to the Congress of People's Deputies?

choices.text: They introduced partly competitive multicandidate contests that were broadcast to a mass public
They immediately ended the Communist Party's legal monopoly by repealing Article 6
choices.note: Article 6 was repealed in 1990, not by the 1989 elections themselves.

choices.text: They created a fully sovereign presidency for the Russian republic
choices.note: The Russian presidency was created later, in 1991.

choices.text: They abolished all reserved seats for party and social organizations
choices.note: Reserved seats remained, which is why the contest was only partly competitive.

questions.explanation: The 1989 elections did not create full democracy, but they opened a major breach in the Soviet system. Televised campaigns and debates gave reformers and critics a national platform and exposed political conflict to millions of viewers.

questions.prompt: In late Soviet politics, what did the 'sovereignty parade' refer to?

choices.text: Union republics and sometimes autonomous units asserting that their own laws had priority over all-Union laws
A series of ceremonial military marches celebrating Soviet victory in World War II
choices.note: The phrase referred to competing claims of legal and political authority, not public commemorations.

choices.text: A campaign to replace republican leaders with direct appointees from Moscow
choices.note: The trend moved in the opposite direction, away from central control.

choices.text: An agreement among republics to postpone demands for autonomy until after economic reform
choices.note: There was no such coordinated pause; the term described accelerating sovereignty claims.

questions.explanation: The sovereignty parade fed the late Soviet 'war of laws.' As republics claimed legal supremacy on their own territory, the authority of central Soviet institutions eroded rapidly.

questions.prompt: Why did creation of the Russian presidency in 1991 strengthen Boris Yeltsin relative to Mikhail Gorbachev?

choices.text: It gave Yeltsin a direct electoral mandate and an institutional base inside the Soviet Union's largest republic
It allowed Yeltsin to appoint the leaders of all other Soviet republics
choices.note: The Russian presidency did not give Yeltsin formal control over other republics.

choices.text: It abolished the Soviet presidency and transferred all union powers to Russia immediately
choices.note: The Soviet presidency continued to exist until the USSR itself collapsed.

choices.text: It made Yeltsin chairman of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union
choices.note: Yeltsin was not elevated to CPSU leadership by the creation of the Russian presidency.

questions.explanation: Gorbachev headed union institutions, but Yeltsin now possessed democratic legitimacy from a popular election in the RSFSR. That mattered because Russia was the largest and politically most consequential Soviet republic.

questions.prompt: What was the political significance of the Belovezha Accords signed in December 1991?

choices.text: Leaders of Russia, Ukraine, and Belarus declared that the USSR had ceased to exist and created the CIS
They ratified the New Union Treaty that hardliners had tried to block in August 1991
choices.note: The Belovezha Accords did not revive the New Union Treaty; they moved beyond the Soviet Union.

choices.text: They placed the Soviet armed forces under permanent command of Gorbachev
choices.note: The accords undercut Gorbachev rather than restoring his authority.

choices.text: They reversed the results of Ukraine's independence referendum
choices.note: Ukraine's referendum had just strongly endorsed independence, which the accords effectively recognized.

questions.explanation: The accords were a decisive political act by republican leaders, not just a legal footnote. They showed that the USSR was being dissolved from below by its key republics rather than preserved by the union center.

questions.prompt: What did Ukraine's Declaration of State Sovereignty, adopted in July 1990, assert?

choices.text: That Ukraine possessed sovereign authority and that republican law should take precedence on its territory, short of immediate full independence
That Ukraine was immediately leaving the Soviet Union and joining NATO
choices.note: The declaration was not a NATO move and did not itself declare full independence.

choices.text: That the Ukrainian Communist Party was being outlawed at once
choices.note: The declaration was about sovereignty and legal authority, not an immediate party ban.

choices.text: That Ukraine would immediately replace the Soviet ruble with its own national currency
choices.note: The declaration concerned republican legal sovereignty, not monetary policy. Ukraine kept using the Soviet ruble and introduced its own currency (first the karbovanets, later the hryvnia) only after independence.

questions.explanation: The 1990 declaration was an important intermediate step between Soviet republican status and full independence in 1991. It helped establish the legal and political language later used to justify complete statehood.

questions.prompt: Why were televised sessions of the Congress of People's Deputies politically destabilizing for the Soviet regime?

choices.text: They exposed elite conflict in public and gave opposition figures a national audience
They reduced public awareness of politics by replacing news coverage with procedural speeches
choices.note: The broadcasts increased political engagement by letting viewers see conflict inside the system.

choices.text: They were closed to the public and therefore intensified rumor rather than information
choices.note: Their impact came precisely from being visible and widely watched.

choices.text: They prevented republic-level politicians from speaking, strengthening central control
choices.note: The sessions instead opened space for republic leaders and critics of the center.

questions.explanation: For decades Soviet politics had been staged as unanimous and opaque. Once viewers could watch dissent, accusation, and embarrassment live on television, the regime's claim to disciplined unity became much less credible.

questions.prompt: In the late Soviet 'war of laws,' what was the RSFSR doing?

choices.text: Passing republican laws and sovereignty claims that asserted precedence over union legislation
Demanding the abolition of all republican institutions in favor of direct rule from Moscow
choices.note: The RSFSR was asserting its own authority against the union center, not eliminating republican institutions.

choices.text: Requiring every Soviet republic to adopt identical constitutions drafted by Russia
choices.note: There was no such harmonization campaign led by the RSFSR.

choices.text: Restoring one-party rule after the repeal of Article 6
choices.note: The 'war of laws' referred to jurisdictional conflict, not a return to formal one-party monopoly.

questions.explanation: Russia was both the core of the Soviet state and one of the main challengers to it. When the RSFSR asserted its own legal supremacy, it further weakened the institutional coherence of the union.

questions.prompt: Why did the failed August 1991 coup accelerate Ukraine's move to full independence?

choices.text: It discredited the union center and raised fears that hardliners could try to reverse republican autonomy by force
It showed that Gorbachev had regained firm control over the Communist Party and military
choices.note: The coup attempt showed the opposite: the center looked weak and divided.

choices.text: It persuaded most Ukrainian voters that a reformed Soviet Union was now more stable than independence
choices.note: The coup undermined confidence in continued union arrangements.

choices.text: It ended all conflict between Ukrainian national democrats and former communist elites overnight
choices.note: Elite divisions remained, even though support for independence rose sharply.

questions.explanation: Ukraine declared independence on August 24, 1991, just after the coup collapsed. The episode convinced many elites and citizens that relying on the Soviet center was dangerous because hardline reversal remained possible.

questions.prompt: Which electoral system was used for Russia's State Duma in most of the 1990s?

choices.text: A mixed-member system combining party-list seats with single-member districts
A purely proportional system with no district races
choices.note: Russia moved to a fully proportional Duma system later, not in most of the 1990s.

choices.text: A purely single-member district system with no party lists
choices.note: Party-list voting was a major part of the Duma system in the 1990s.

choices.text: Indirect appointment by regional governors and local councils
choices.note: Duma deputies were elected, not delegated by subnational authorities.

questions.explanation: The mixed system helped produce a fragmented parliament: national party lists rewarded emerging parties, while district races gave space to local notables and independents. That fragmentation mattered for the weak institutionalization of Russia's party system.
sources: Daniel Treisman, The Return: Russia's Journey from Gorbachev to Medvedev

questions.prompt: In 1990s Russia, what was a 'party of power'?

choices.text: A pro-Kremlin electoral vehicle built to support incumbents rather than a deeply rooted ideological mass party
A legal term for any party that won an absolute majority in the State Duma
choices.note: The phrase referred to the party's relationship to state power, not to a formal majority threshold.

choices.text: A nationalist opposition party seeking to replace the presidency with military rule
choices.note: A 'party of power' was aligned with the executive, not an anti-system military project.

choices.text: A regional secessionist party demanding dissolution of the federation
choices.note: These parties were created to support the center, not dismantle it.

questions.explanation: Russia's early post-Soviet party politics featured repeated attempts to build top-down pro-government vehicles. Their weakness showed how little Russia had developed stable, programmatic parties during the 1990s.
sources: Daniel Treisman, The Return: Russia's Journey from Gorbachev to Medvedev

questions.prompt: Why did Russia's 1993 constitution create a durable executive advantage over parliament?

choices.text: It gave the president strong decree and agenda-setting powers, plus important leverage over government formation and dissolution of the Duma
It transformed Russia into a parliamentary republic where the prime minister depended only on coalition bargaining
choices.note: The 1993 constitution did not subordinate the presidency to parliament; it strengthened presidential authority.

choices.text: It made regional governors collectively more powerful than the federal executive
choices.note: The constitution did not place governors above the presidency.

choices.text: It required all federal laws to be approved first by the Constitutional Court
choices.note: Judicial review existed, but this was not the core institutional shift that advantaged the executive.

questions.explanation: The constitution emerged directly from the 1993 crisis and encoded a powerful presidency. Many analysts see it as a key institutional foundation for the later centralization of Russian politics.
sources: Daniel Treisman, The Return: Russia's Journey from Gorbachev to Medvedev

questions.prompt: Why were independents and loose electoral blocs so prominent in 1990s Ukrainian parliamentary politics?

choices.text: Because party institutionalization was weak and many politicians relied on regional, personal, and business networks instead
Because Ukrainian law banned formal political parties from contesting parliamentary elections
choices.note: Political parties were legal; the issue was their weakness and fragmentation.

choices.text: Because the presidency allocated parliamentary seats directly to governors and mayors
choices.note: Seats were contested electorally, not distributed by presidential appointment.

choices.text: Because Ukraine required all candidates to run on a single national list approved by the cabinet
choices.note: Ukraine's system did not operate through one government-approved national slate.

questions.explanation: Weak party organization was a central feature of Ukraine's early post-Soviet politics. It helped make parliament fluid, coalition building unstable, and regional bargaining especially important.
sources: Maria Popova & Oxana Shevel, Russia and Ukraine: Entangled Histories, Diverging States

questions.prompt: What made Tatarstan an emblem of Russia's asymmetrical federalism in the 1990s?

choices.text: Its 1994 bilateral treaty went further than Moscow's deals with other regions, recognizing Tatarstan's claims to sovereignty and control over its own budget, taxes, and natural resources
It became an independent state recognized by the United Nations while staying inside Russia's customs area
choices.note: Tatarstan did not gain international independence or UN membership.

choices.text: It replaced federal law entirely with Islamic law under a separate constitution
choices.note: Tatarstan pushed for broad autonomy, but not a full replacement of the Russian legal order in this way.

choices.text: It was the only Russian region allowed to elect its own president after 1993
choices.note: The distinctive feature was its treaty-based autonomy, not exclusive access to elections.

questions.explanation: Yeltsin signed bilateral power-sharing treaties with dozens of regions in the 1990s, but Tatarstan's 1994 treaty was the most far-reaching. It acknowledged Tatarstan as a state "associated with" Russia, handed it substantial fiscal powers, and gave it control over key economic assets. That made it the signature case of asymmetrical federalism, where some regions extracted much better deals than others.

questions.prompt: In the 1990s and early 2000s, which area formed the strongest electoral base for politicians like Viktor Yanukovych?

choices.text: Eastern and southeastern industrial regions, especially the Donbas
Galicia and the far west of Ukraine
choices.note: The west was generally much less supportive of Yanukovych and similar politicians.

choices.text: Only Crimea, with almost no support elsewhere
choices.note: Crimea mattered, but Yanukovych's base extended well beyond it into the east and southeast.

choices.text: Mainly rural central regions around Kyiv and Vinnytsia
choices.note: Those areas were not the core of his durable electoral base.

questions.explanation: Regional voting patterns became a major feature of Ukrainian politics, though they were never the whole story. Donbas in particular mattered because of its industrial weight, networks of local power, and symbolic role in east-south politics.
sources: Maria Popova & Oxana Shevel, Russia and Ukraine: Entangled Histories, Diverging States

questions.prompt: By the end of the 1990s, which statement best characterized Russia's and Ukraine's executive systems?

choices.text: Both were formally semi-presidential, but Russia's 1993 constitution gave the presidency markedly stronger formal powers on paper, even though Yeltsin's actual political authority remained constrained throughout the decade
Yeltsin had already centralized power to a degree comparable with Putin's later system, while Kuchma's presidency in Ukraine remained essentially ceremonial
choices.note: Through the end of the 1990s Yeltsin was politically weak — arguably weaker than Kuchma — and centralized presidential power in Russia was a 2000s development under Putin.

choices.text: Russia had no meaningful federal institutions, while Ukraine was formally a federation of republics
choices.note: Russia remained a federation; Ukraine remained a unitary state.

choices.text: Both countries had converged on nearly identical party systems and executive-legislative relations
choices.note: Their institutional trajectories diverged, even though both were formally semi-presidential on paper.

questions.explanation: Russia and Ukraine were both formally semi-presidential systems after their mid-1990s constitutions, but Russia's 1993 text assigned the presidency notably stronger formal powers. In practice, Yeltsin's authority was limited through the 1990s — bargaining with regional governors, the Duma, and oligarchs — and recentralization is a Putin-era phenomenon. The contrast that mattered was the design of the constitutional framework, not the raw strength of the person in office.
sources: Maria Popova & Oxana Shevel, Russia and Ukraine: Entangled Histories, Diverging States

questions.prompt: Why was Crimea a recurring state-building challenge for Kyiv in the 1990s?

choices.text: Because its autonomous status, Russian-majority population, and assertive local elites created repeated disputes over how much self-rule it should have inside Ukraine
Because Crimea had already been annexed by Russia in the early 1990s
choices.note: Russia annexed Crimea in 2014, not in the 1990s.

choices.text: Because Crimea was constitutionally the capital of independent Ukraine until 1996
choices.note: Kyiv was the capital; Crimea's issue was autonomy and jurisdiction.

choices.text: Because Crimea was the center of Ukraine's strongest nationalist movement for linguistic Ukrainization
choices.note: Crimea was not the main base of Ukrainian national-democratic mobilization.

questions.explanation: Crimea showed that Ukraine's regional politics were not only about elections; they also involved institutional questions of autonomy, identity, and control over territory. Kyiv ultimately kept Crimea inside a unitary state but never fully eliminated the issue.
sources: Maria Popova & Oxana Shevel, Russia and Ukraine: Entangled Histories, Diverging States

questions.prompt: What best distinguished Russia's center-periphery bargaining from Ukraine's regional politics in the 1990s?

choices.text: Russia bargained with constitutionally entrenched federal subjects through asymmetrical treaties, while Ukraine was formally unitary and handled regional conflict more through electoral and autonomy disputes, above all in Crimea
Russia had no subnational bargaining at all, while Ukraine was a loose confederation of nearly sovereign regions
choices.note: Russia's 1990s politics involved extensive subnational bargaining, and Ukraine was not a confederation.

choices.text: Both countries solved regional conflict by abolishing local elections and appointing all mayors from the center
choices.note: Neither country followed that exact path in the 1990s.

choices.text: Ukraine's regions possessed more formal treaty-based sovereignty than Russia's republics
choices.note: Treaty-based asymmetry is much more associated with Russia in the Yeltsin era.

questions.explanation: The distinction mattered for later institutional development. Russia's problem was heavily about federal bargaining with strong regional units, while Ukraine's was more about how regional identity and local power affected a unitary state.
sources: Maria Popova & Oxana Shevel, Russia and Ukraine: Entangled Histories, Diverging States

questions.prompt: What was the basic logic of the Putin-Medvedev 'tandem' that began in 2008?

choices.text: Medvedev became president while Putin became prime minister, but Putin remained the dominant political figure
Putin retired fully from politics and transferred his support base to Medvedev permanently
choices.note: The tandem was not a full transfer of power away from Putin.

choices.text: Russia divided the presidency between Medvedev for domestic affairs and Putin for foreign policy by constitutional amendment
choices.note: No such constitutional dual presidency was created.

choices.text: Medvedev led the opposition while Putin headed the ruling party from the presidency
choices.note: The tandem operated within the ruling camp, not as a government-opposition split.

questions.explanation: The arrangement showed how formal offices and real political power could diverge in Russia. It also previewed the regime's reliance on managed succession rather than open uncertainty over who would rule.
sources: Daniel Treisman, The Return: Russia's Journey from Gorbachev to Medvedev

questions.prompt: What was a major political consequence of Russia's move to a fully proportional system for State Duma elections in 2007?

choices.text: It disadvantaged independents and smaller regional candidates while favoring large national parties, especially United Russia
It guaranteed equal parliamentary representation to every federal subject regardless of population
choices.note: That would describe a territorial chamber, not the Duma's party-list system.

choices.text: It restored one-party elections by legally banning all opposition parties
choices.note: Opposition parties were not formally abolished, even though the playing field became much less fair.

choices.text: It transferred control of Duma seat allocation from voters to regional governors
choices.note: Seats still came through elections, not direct gubernatorial assignment.

questions.explanation: Electoral-system change worked together with media control and administrative pressure. The result was not just a technical reform but a more manageable party system for the Kremlin.
sources: Daniel Treisman, The Return: Russia's Journey from Gorbachev to Medvedev

questions.prompt: Why did the Yukos affair have major political importance beyond business regulation?

choices.text: It signaled that oligarchs could not act as autonomous political centers and that the Kremlin would use state power selectively against them
It marked Russia's first peaceful alternation of presidential power through free elections
choices.note: The Yukos affair was about state-business relations and power, not an electoral alternation.

choices.text: It decentralized control over the oil sector by transferring key assets to regional governments
choices.note: The affair became associated with centralization, not regional devolution.

choices.text: It led to a constitutional court ruling that permanently weakened the presidency
choices.note: The broader political message of the case ran in the opposite direction.

questions.explanation: The case against Mikhail Khodorkovsky helped redefine the terms of elite politics in Putin's Russia. Wealth alone was not the issue; the key point was that independent political influence outside Kremlin control had become dangerous.
sources: Daniel Treisman, The Return: Russia's Journey from Gorbachev to Medvedev

questions.prompt: Why did President Viktor Yushchenko dissolve Ukraine's parliament in 2007?

choices.text: He argued that coalition defections were creating an illegitimate majority and threatening the post-Orange constitutional balance
He was constitutionally required to call a new election after losing the 2004 repeat runoff
choices.note: The 2004 repeat runoff had already been resolved years earlier and won by Yushchenko.

choices.text: He dissolved parliament because Ukraine had voted to replace the presidency with a monarchy
choices.note: No such constitutional transformation occurred.

choices.text: He was carrying out a Supreme Court order to annul all political parties formed after 1991
choices.note: There was no such order; the dispute centered on parliamentary majorities and defections.

questions.explanation: The 2007 crisis showed how the post-Orange institutional design still produced struggles over where legitimate authority lay. Ukraine remained competitive, but its constitutional order was unstable and repeatedly contested.
sources: Maria Popova & Oxana Shevel, Russia and Ukraine: Entangled Histories, Diverging States

questions.prompt: What happened to regional governors in Russia's Federation Council after Putin's reform in 2000?

choices.text: They no longer sat there ex officio; their regions instead sent designated representatives
They were all promoted into the federal cabinet as ministers without portfolio
choices.note: The reform reduced governors' direct federal influence rather than turning them into ministers.

choices.text: They became directly elected members of the State Duma
choices.note: The Federation Council and State Duma are different chambers, and this was not the reform.

choices.text: They gained the right to veto presidential decrees as a bloc
choices.note: The reform did not create a new collective veto for governors.

questions.explanation: Removing governors from the Federation Council was part of Putin's early recentralization. It weakened a channel through which powerful regional leaders had exercised direct influence at the federal level.

questions.prompt: What was the 'anti-crisis coalition' formed in Ukraine in 2006?

choices.text: An alliance of the Party of Regions, Socialists, and Communists that brought Viktor Yanukovych back as prime minister
A wartime cabinet formed after Russia's annexation of Crimea
choices.note: That would refer to events in 2014, not to the 2006 coalition.

choices.text: A temporary nonpartisan government installed by the Supreme Court during the Orange Revolution
choices.note: The anti-crisis coalition was a parliamentary alliance, not a court-installed technocratic cabinet.

choices.text: A pact between Yushchenko and Tymoshenko to dissolve all oligarch-owned media outlets
choices.note: The term referred to coalition formation in parliament, not a media policy agreement.

questions.explanation: The coalition underscored how fluid Ukrainian parliamentary alignments could be. It also demonstrated that the Orange camp had not created a stable, unified post-revolutionary governing bloc.

questions.prompt: Before United Russia stabilized, why did Kremlin-backed electoral vehicles repeatedly appear under new names such as Our Home Is Russia and Unity?

choices.text: Because they were top-down incumbent projects tied to shifting elite coalitions, not durable programmatic mass parties
Because Russian law required every governing party to dissolve after a single election cycle
choices.note: No such automatic dissolution rule explains the pattern.

choices.text: Because the Constitutional Court banned any party from supporting the sitting president twice in a row
choices.note: There was no such ban.

choices.text: Because tax and registration rules favored newly created parties, making rebranding cheaper than sustaining an existing organization
choices.note: Registration rules did not actively reward relaunches; the turnover reflected elite strategy and weak institutionalization, not regulatory incentives.

questions.explanation: The repeated rebranding showed that these parties were often instruments of the executive, not independent organizations with loyal memberships and clear ideologies. United Russia later mattered because it gave that project a more durable shell.
sources: Daniel Treisman, The Return: Russia's Journey from Gorbachev to Medvedev

questions.prompt: Why did Russia's governors become less autonomous in the 2000s while Ukraine's regional bosses remained politically consequential?

choices.text: Russia built a tighter presidential hierarchy and administrative vertical, while Ukraine's divided institutions and competitive politics forced continued bargaining with regional power brokers
Ukraine abolished all subnational politics after 2004, whereas Russia democratized regional elections
choices.note: This reverses the broad contrast; Russia centralized more, while Ukraine remained more regionally contested.

choices.text: Both countries adopted identical decentralization packages, but Russia's was better funded
choices.note: Their institutional trajectories in this period were notably different, not parallel.

choices.text: Regional bosses disappeared from Ukrainian politics because parties became fully ideological and nationally uniform
choices.note: Regional networks remained important in Ukraine.

questions.explanation: This contrast helps explain broader regime divergence. Putin's Russia increasingly subordinated regional elites to the center, while Ukraine's fragmented competition kept regional machines relevant to national politics.
sources: Maria Popova & Oxana Shevel, Russia and Ukraine: Entangled Histories, Diverging States

questions.prompt: Which statement best captures the difference between ruling parties in 2000s Russia and Ukraine?

choices.text: United Russia served as a dominant party organizing executive control, while Ukraine had powerful parties but no single nationwide machine that could monopolize politics in the same way
Ukraine's ruling parties were more centralized than Russia's because Kyiv directly appointed all party leaders in the regions
choices.note: Ukraine's party system was not organized through a single centralized dominant-party machine.

choices.text: Both countries had nearly identical dominant-party systems by the mid-2000s
choices.note: A key comparative point is that Ukraine did not consolidate a Russia-style dominant-party order.

choices.text: Russia had no meaningful ruling party because the presidency was constitutionally forbidden from using parties at all
choices.note: United Russia became a central instrument of regime management.

questions.explanation: The contrast was not simply authoritarian versus democratic. It also involved how political organization worked: Russia increasingly fused party and executive power, whereas Ukraine's competition remained fragmented and less monopolized.
sources: Maria Popova & Oxana Shevel, Russia and Ukraine: Entangled Histories, Diverging States

questions.prompt: What most directly triggered Russia's 2011-2012 protest wave?

choices.text: Allegations of fraud in the 2011 Duma elections, reinforced by anger over Putin's planned return to the presidency
The abolition of the Federation Council in favor of a unicameral parliament
choices.note: Russia did not abolish the Federation Council in 2011.

choices.text: A failed military coup against Dmitry Medvedev
choices.note: The protests were not sparked by a coup attempt.

choices.text: Russia's sudden decision to recognize Crimea as an independent state
choices.note: Crimea became central to Russian politics later, in 2014.

questions.explanation: The protests marked the largest urban anti-regime mobilization of Putin's era to that point. They were especially strong among educated city dwellers who viewed elections as manipulated and succession as prearranged.
sources: M. Steven Fish, What is Putinism?

questions.prompt: In Russian gubernatorial politics, what is the 'municipal filter'?

choices.text: A rule requiring candidates to collect support signatures from municipal deputies or local officials before they can run
A law that limits voting in governor's races to residents of major cities
choices.note: The municipal filter concerns candidate registration, not who may vote.

choices.text: A constitutional clause giving mayors the power to veto gubernatorial appointments
choices.note: It is not a mayoral veto device.

choices.text: A media regulation that bars governors from appearing on local television during campaigns
choices.note: The term refers to ballot access rules, not broadcast restrictions.

questions.explanation: The municipal filter is politically important because it can screen out opposition candidates before voters even cast ballots. It is a good example of how formally electoral institutions can still be used to narrow competition.

questions.prompt: What was a major institutional goal of Ukraine's post-2014 decentralization reform?

choices.text: To devolve more resources and some governing authority to consolidated local communities while keeping Ukraine a unitary state
To convert Ukraine into a federation with constitutionally sovereign provinces
choices.note: The reform was not a move to federalization.

choices.text: To abolish local self-government and return most budgeting powers to Kyiv ministries
choices.note: The reform moved in the opposite direction, toward stronger local capacity.

choices.text: To replace elected local councils with military governors in every region
choices.note: That was not the general institutional design of decentralization.

questions.explanation: Decentralization became a major post-Euromaidan state-building project. Supporters argued it could strengthen governance and local accountability without conceding the federalization demanded by some outside actors.
sources: Maria Popova & Oxana Shevel, Russia and Ukraine: Entangled Histories, Diverging States

questions.prompt: What was a central feature of Ukraine's 2015 decommunization laws?

choices.text: They mandated the removal or renaming of many Soviet-era symbols and places and promoted access to formerly closed archives
They imposed automatic lustration on all judges and prosecutors who had served before 1991, removing them from office
choices.note: Lustration of officials was the subject of separate Ukrainian laws (notably the 2014 lustration law), not the 2015 decommunization package.

choices.text: They centralized all monument-removal decisions in the federal Ministry of Culture, requiring its approval for each individual case
choices.note: Implementation of the symbol-removal provisions was largely delegated to local authorities working with the Ukrainian Institute of National Memory, not concentrated in a single federal ministry.

choices.text: They abolished Ukraine's Victory Day commemoration and replaced it with a national mourning day
choices.note: The laws renamed and rescheduled the commemoration (introducing May 8 as the Day of Remembrance and Reconciliation alongside May 9) but did not abolish the holiday.

questions.explanation: The laws were part of a broader post-2014 effort to redefine public memory and state identity. They were politically significant because they linked nation-building to street names, monuments, and archival access.

questions.prompt: What was most unusual about Volodymyr Zelensky's 2019 presidential victory?

choices.text: He won as a political outsider and entertainer with no prior elected office
He was the first sitting Ukrainian president ever to win reelection
choices.note: Zelensky was not an incumbent president in 2019.

choices.text: He became president after being selected by the military rather than by voters
choices.note: He won through a national election.

choices.text: He ran as the official candidate of the Party of Regions machine in Donbas
choices.note: Zelensky was not the Party of Regions successor candidate.

questions.explanation: Zelensky's victory showed how open Ukraine's electoral arena remained. It also reflected deep voter frustration with established elites and the continuing volatility of Ukrainian party and presidential politics.
sources: Olga Onuch and Henry Hale, The Zelensky Effect

questions.prompt: After 2014, what happened to the old east-west electoral map that had structured much of Ukrainian politics?

choices.text: It was weakened substantially as war, the loss of Crimea, partial loss of Donbas, and new party competition reshaped the old regional pattern
It disappeared overnight because all regional voting differences in Ukraine ended completely
choices.note: Regional variation persisted, even if the old map became less dominant.

choices.text: It became stronger than ever because the same parties controlled exactly the same territories as before 2014
choices.note: The post-2014 environment disrupted rather than perfectly reproduced the earlier pattern.

choices.text: It was replaced by a formal constitutional division of Ukraine into eastern and western electoral zones
choices.note: No such constitutional split was adopted.

questions.explanation: Earlier Ukrainian politics often revolved around a durable east-west cleavage, but 2014 changed the geography of competition. Territorial loss, war, and party-system change made the old map less predictive than it had once been.
sources: Maria Popova & Oxana Shevel, Russia and Ukraine: Entangled Histories, Diverging States

questions.prompt: In her article 'Why the Orange Revolution Was Short and Peaceful and Euromaidan Long and Violent,' Maria Popova argues that the key reason for the contrast between Ukraine's two protest waves was:

choices.text: In Popova's account, the 2004 regime fragmented and hesitated earlier, while by 2013-2014 the state had a more cohesive coercive apparatus and used force more aggressively
Because Russia had already annexed Crimea in 2004, forcing protesters to compromise quickly
choices.note: Russia annexed Crimea in 2014, not during the Orange Revolution.

choices.text: Because Orange protesters refused elections and demanded immediate abolition of the presidency
choices.note: The Orange movement centered on electoral fraud and a repeat vote, not abolition of the office itself.

choices.text: Because Euromaidan lacked support outside Kyiv while the Orange Revolution was nationwide
choices.note: Euromaidan also drew support beyond Kyiv; Popova's comparison focuses more on elite fragmentation and state coercion.

questions.explanation: Popova emphasizes differences in elite cohesion and the state's willingness and capacity to use coercion. Her comparison is not mainly about protester motives, but about how the regime responded in 2004 versus 2013-2014.
sources: Maria Popova, Why the Orange Revolution Was Short and Peaceful and Euromaidan Long and Violent

questions.prompt: Which institutional contrast best captures Russia and Ukraine in the 2010s?

choices.text: Russia deepened a centralized authoritarian vertical, while Ukraine retained competitive national elections and pursued decentralization despite war and crisis
Both countries converged on the same dominant-party presidential model after 2014
choices.note: Their trajectories remained sharply different rather than converging on one model.

choices.text: Ukraine abandoned elections entirely, while Russia devolved broad fiscal autonomy to local governments
choices.note: This reverses the broad pattern of the decade.

choices.text: Russia and Ukraine both replaced their presidencies with ceremonial heads of state and coalition cabinets
choices.note: Neither country made that institutional shift.

questions.explanation: The contrast is a central comparative theme. Russia continued to centralize authority around a narrow executive core, while Ukraine remained more electorally open and institutionally plural even under severe external pressure.
sources: Maria Popova & Oxana Shevel, Russia and Ukraine: Entangled Histories, Diverging States

questions.prompt: In the 1920s, Soviet nationality policy known as 'korenizatsiya' ('indigenization') promoted which approach in non-Russian republics like Ukraine?

choices.text: Expanding local-language education, publishing, and administration and recruiting national cadres into party and state posts
Forced population transfers designed to make each republic ethnically Russian
choices.note: That approach is associated with later Stalinist deportations, not the 1920s indigenization program.

choices.text: Abolishing the federal structure of the USSR in favor of a single unitary state
choices.note: The 1920s policy worked through, not against, the federal republic structure; the USSR was formally constituted as a federation in 1922.

choices.text: Prohibiting any use of languages other than Russian in official life
choices.note: Russification of that scope came later, especially from the 1930s onward.

questions.explanation: Korenizatsiya was the Bolsheviks' early bid to win over non-Russian populations by supporting national languages, schools, and elites within republic boundaries. It helped consolidate republic-level identities — notably in Ukraine — before being reversed and, in the 1930s, replaced by repression of national cultural elites.

questions.prompt: What did Khrushchev's 1956 'Secret Speech' to the 20th Party Congress do?

choices.text: Openly denounced Stalin's cult of personality and mass repressions, launching a limited period of de-Stalinization
Rehabilitated Leon Trotsky and the defendants of the Moscow Show Trials as innocent victims of Stalin
choices.note: The speech did not rehabilitate Trotsky; Khrushchev criticized Stalin's treatment of loyal party members but kept the Bolshevik-era verdicts against intra-party opponents like Trotsky in place.

choices.text: Proclaimed the doctrine of 'peaceful coexistence' with the capitalist West
choices.note: Peaceful coexistence was also advanced by Khrushchev around the 20th Party Congress but was a separate element of the Congress, not the content of the Secret Speech.

choices.text: Announced the launch of the Virgin Lands agricultural campaign
choices.note: The Virgin Lands campaign was an earlier 1954 agricultural initiative associated with Khrushchev; it was not the subject of the 1956 Secret Speech.

questions.explanation: Delivered behind closed doors but quickly leaked, the speech shook communist parties worldwide. It enabled the release of many Gulag prisoners, a partial cultural thaw, and a reframing of Stalin as an individual deviation rather than a product of the system — a framing that shaped reform debates into the Gorbachev era.

questions.prompt: How did the 1986 Chernobyl nuclear disaster contribute politically to the Soviet system's later unraveling?

choices.text: It exposed the regime's habit of secrecy and denial, bolstered demands for glasnost, and energized Ukrainian environmental and national movements
It triggered an immediate multiparty transition and free elections within months
choices.note: Multiparty politics in the USSR emerged only after the repeal of Article 6 in 1990, and free republic-level elections followed later.

choices.text: It led Gorbachev to abandon perestroika and return to orthodox central planning
choices.note: Gorbachev instead expanded perestroika and glasnost in the years after Chernobyl.

choices.text: It prompted Ukraine to declare full independence that same year
choices.note: Ukraine's Declaration of State Sovereignty came in July 1990 and its independence vote in 1991, years later.

questions.explanation: The regime's initial cover-up of the Chernobyl accident — a meltdown at a reactor in Soviet Ukraine — clashed badly with Gorbachev's emerging openness campaign. It strengthened civic movements, especially in Ukraine, where environmental protest became an early vehicle for nationally coded political mobilization.

questions.prompt: The 'Novo-Ogaryovo process' or 'Nine-Plus-One' agreement of spring 1991 was an attempt to:

choices.text: Negotiate a new voluntary union treaty between Gorbachev and nine willing Soviet republics to replace the existing USSR structure
Merge the Russian and Ukrainian republics into a single larger unit within the USSR
choices.note: Novo-Ogaryovo aimed at redesigning the whole union, not merging specific republics.

choices.text: Transfer all Soviet nuclear weapons from the republics to Russia before dissolution
choices.note: That consolidation was handled through later post-1991 arrangements, not the Novo-Ogaryovo talks.

choices.text: Create a formal role for the Communist Party in the governance of each republic
choices.note: The process was about union-republic relations and confederal bargaining, not Party role.

questions.explanation: At Gorbachev's Novo-Ogaryovo estate, nine participating republics and the Soviet center drafted a looser union treaty intended to keep most of the USSR together. The treaty was to be signed in late August 1991, and the August coup was triggered in part by hardline opposition to that signing.

questions.prompt: The 1989 'Baltic Way' was a mass protest in which:

choices.text: About two million people joined hands in a human chain across Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania to mark the 50th anniversary of the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact
Baltic workers launched a nationwide strike demanding entry into NATO
choices.note: NATO membership was not on the Baltic agenda in 1989 and came only after Soviet collapse.

choices.text: The three Baltic parliaments jointly declared immediate independence from the USSR
choices.note: The Baltic declarations of sovereignty and independence came on separate dates in 1990 and 1991, not during the 1989 demonstration.

choices.text: Soviet authorities organized a pro-union march to counter rising separatist sentiment
choices.note: The Baltic Way was a grassroots protest movement against Soviet rule, not a state-organized rally.

questions.explanation: The August 23, 1989 human chain marked the 50th anniversary of the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact's secret protocols that had assigned the Baltic states to the Soviet sphere. By framing Soviet rule as the product of a discredited Nazi-Soviet deal, the protest put the legitimacy of their incorporation into the USSR at the center of the debate.

questions.prompt: What triggered Russia's First Chechen War, which began in December 1994?

choices.text: Moscow sent federal troops to reimpose control over the self-declared Chechen Republic of Ichkeria after years of de facto independence under Dzhokhar Dudayev
A formal NATO intervention on the side of Chechen separatists
choices.note: No NATO intervention occurred in Chechnya; the war was fought between federal Russian forces and Chechen fighters.

choices.text: A Russian referendum in which a majority voted to abolish Chechnya as a federal subject
choices.note: There was no such referendum; Moscow's move was an executive military decision by Yeltsin's government.

choices.text: The signing of a power-sharing treaty between Moscow and Grozny similar to Tatarstan's
choices.note: Unlike Tatarstan, Chechnya refused Moscow's terms; the bilateral-treaty route was the path not taken.

questions.explanation: Chechnya had declared independence in 1991 under Dudayev and largely operated outside federal control. Yeltsin's decision to use force in late 1994 aimed to reassert central authority but produced a grinding, unpopular war that ended with the 1996 Khasavyurt accords and left Chechnya's status unresolved.

questions.prompt: Which two figures led Russia's parliamentary side of the October 1993 standoff with President Yeltsin?

choices.text: Speaker Ruslan Khasbulatov and Vice-President Alexander Rutskoi
Prime Minister Viktor Chernomyrdin and Finance Minister Yegor Gaidar
choices.note: Chernomyrdin was Yeltsin's prime minister and Gaidar was associated with the government's reform team; neither led the parliamentary opposition.

choices.text: Communist Party leader Gennady Zyuganov and LDPR leader Vladimir Zhirinovsky
choices.note: Zyuganov and Zhirinovsky became prominent through the December 1993 elections, not as leaders of the armed parliamentary side in the October showdown.

choices.text: General Alexander Lebed and oligarch Boris Berezovsky
choices.note: Lebed was a military figure who became politically prominent later, and Berezovsky was a financier, not a leader of the Supreme Soviet.

questions.explanation: Vice-President Rutskoi and Supreme Soviet Speaker Khasbulatov became the figureheads of the parliamentary resistance when Yeltsin dissolved the legislature. After supporters attacked a television center, Yeltsin ordered tanks to shell the White House; both leaders were arrested and later amnestied.

questions.prompt: What made the results of Russia's December 1993 State Duma elections politically striking?

choices.text: Vladimir Zhirinovsky's nationalist Liberal Democratic Party (LDPR) won a surprisingly large share of the party-list vote, signaling the strength of a populist-nationalist opposition to Yeltsin's course
The Communist Party was officially disqualified and excluded from the ballot
choices.note: The Communist Party of the Russian Federation participated and became a leading opposition party through the 1990s.

choices.text: Russia's Choice, Yeltsin's allied bloc, won a parliamentary supermajority
choices.note: Russia's Choice actually underperformed expectations; the elections did not produce a pro-Yeltsin supermajority.

choices.text: Voter turnout exceeded 90 percent, the highest recorded in any Russian election
choices.note: Turnout was substantial but not near that level; the memorable story was the LDPR's share, not a turnout record.

questions.explanation: The 1993 election coincided with the new constitution's referendum and showcased the appeal of anti-reform nationalism. Zhirinovsky's strong performance — running ahead of pro-government parties on the party list — was widely read as a backlash against shock therapy and the October 1993 violence.

questions.prompt: Why did Yeltsin's appointment of Yevgeny Primakov as prime minister in September 1998 matter politically?

choices.text: Primakov had broad support across the Duma after the August 1998 crash and represented a pivot away from the reform-minded technocrats associated with the crisis
Primakov formally abolished the Russian presidency during his tenure
choices.note: The presidency remained in place; Primakov served under Yeltsin as prime minister.

choices.text: He ran as Yeltsin's chosen successor and won a snap presidential election in 1999
choices.note: Primakov was dismissed by Yeltsin in 1999, and Putin — not Primakov — emerged as Yeltsin's successor.

choices.text: He was Russia's first prime minister drawn from the Communist Party
choices.note: Primakov was not a Communist Party PM in that sense; he was a foreign-policy insider acceptable to the Duma's communist-led majority.

questions.explanation: After the ruble crash and Kiriyenko's ouster, Yeltsin — politically weakened — accepted Primakov, a figure acceptable to the opposition-dominated Duma. His government stabilized the economy and enjoyed broad public support, to the point that Yeltsin sacked him in May 1999 as Primakov's independent stature began to look threatening.

questions.prompt: Why is former Ukrainian prime minister Pavlo Lazarenko a notable figure in 1990s Ukrainian politics?

choices.text: He served as prime minister in 1996-1997 and was later convicted in a United States court of laundering funds tied to Ukrainian gas-trade schemes, illustrating the intertwining of energy business and high office under Kuchma
He founded Rukh and led Ukraine's democratic opposition in the early 1990s
choices.note: Rukh was founded by dissident and cultural figures, notably Viacheslav Chornovil; Lazarenko was not its founder.

choices.text: He served two terms as Ukraine's president between Kravchuk and Kuchma
choices.note: Ukraine's presidential sequence in the 1990s was Kravchuk then Kuchma; Lazarenko was prime minister, not president.

choices.text: He negotiated Ukraine's 1994 Budapest Memorandum on security assurances
choices.note: The Budapest Memorandum was negotiated at the presidential level under Kravchuk and Kuchma; Lazarenko was not its lead negotiator.

questions.explanation: Lazarenko's career showed how control over Ukraine's gas-trading infrastructure — especially United Energy Systems, associated with Yulia Tymoshenko — could translate into both private fortune and top state office. His later U.S. conviction for money laundering became a widely cited emblem of the era's oligarchic-state fusion.

questions.prompt: The post-Stalin 'Thaw' (Ottepel'), associated chiefly with Khrushchev's leadership, is best described as:

choices.text: A period of partial liberalization — mass release of Gulag prisoners, looser censorship that allowed works like Solzhenitsyn's One Day in the Life of Ivan Denisovich, and a move away from mass terror — while one-party rule and central planning continued
A constitutional reform that introduced competing candidates in Soviet elections within a one-party framework
choices.note: Soviet elections remained uncontested single-candidate affairs throughout the Khrushchev period; competitive elements appeared only under Gorbachev.

choices.text: A revival of Lenin's New Economic Policy that legalized private trade and small household businesses
choices.note: Khrushchev-era economic experiments (sovnarkhoz decentralization, the Virgin Lands campaign) stayed within the state-socialist framework; there was no legal private trade.

choices.text: A wholesale rehabilitation of all Bolshevik opponents purged by Stalin, including Trotsky and Bukharin
choices.note: Khrushchev-era rehabilitations targeted loyal Party members executed in the 1930s purges; Bukharin's rehabilitation came only under Gorbachev, and Trotsky's Bolshevik-era verdicts were never reversed.

questions.explanation: The Thaw took its name from Ilya Ehrenburg's 1954 novella. Its hallmarks were the release of millions of political prisoners after Stalin's death, limited space for previously censored literature and film, and the end of mass arrests — while the Party's monopoly on politics, the planned economy, and Marxist-Leninist ideology remained firmly in place. The period ended with Khrushchev's 1964 ouster and a partial 'refreeze' under Brezhnev.
sources: Archie Brown, The Rise and Fall of Communism

questions.prompt: Classic political-science definitions of Stalinist totalitarianism typically identify which four core features?

choices.text: Mass terror, an all-encompassing official ideology, the leading role of a single party, and a command economy
A secret-police apparatus, forced-labor camps, show trials, and a cult of personality
choices.note: These were defining instruments of Stalinism, but the classical definition of totalitarianism names structural features (ideology, single party, terror as system, planned economy) rather than specific mechanisms.

choices.text: Ethnic nationalism, militarist mobilization, imperial expansion, and charismatic leadership
choices.note: That set maps more closely to fascism — especially Nazism — than to Stalinist totalitarianism, whose official ideology was universalist and class-based rather than racial-national.

choices.text: Absolute monarchy, hereditary aristocracy, an established state church, and serf-based agriculture
choices.note: The Soviet state was defined in direct opposition to those pre-revolutionary features, which it set out to destroy.

questions.explanation: The four-feature definition frames totalitarianism as a distinctive system — not merely a harsh dictatorship — combining a monopoly party, an official worldview, routinized terror, and centrally planned economic allocation. Institutional-complementarity arguments about Soviet collapse build on this framing: once Gorbachev weakened any one of the four pillars (especially the Party's leading role and the ideology's grip), the others destabilized together.
sources: Gans-Morse, Explaining the Collapse of the Soviet System (course handout)

questions.prompt: The March 1989 elections to the USSR Congress of People's Deputies broke with past Soviet practice in a specific way. What was distinctive about them?

choices.text: For the first time in decades, most seats featured contested, multi-candidate races (though one-third were still reserved for seats chosen by 'public organizations' including the Communist Party)
They were the first fully free elections in Russia, with all seats contested and the Communist Party formally disqualified from running
choices.note: Article 6, which guaranteed the Party's leading role, was not repealed until 1990. The 1989 elections still had reserved seats for the CPSU and other official organizations, and the Party was not banned from running.

choices.text: They were the first Soviet elections to use secret ballots, a practice previously banned by Stalin and left unreformed
choices.note: Soviet elections had used secret ballots for decades; the innovation in 1989 was contestation, not balloting procedure.

choices.text: They introduced proportional representation based on the Ukrainian Rukh electoral model adopted across the union
choices.note: The 1989 Congress used majoritarian territorial and national-territorial districts plus reserved 'public organization' seats, not proportional representation, and Rukh did not provide an electoral template for the union.

questions.explanation: The 1989 Congress of People's Deputies elections were a signature glasnost-era reform: genuine contested voting for most seats, producing a legislature that included prominent critics of the regime like Andrei Sakharov and Boris Yeltsin. One-third of seats were still reserved for 'public organizations' (the CPSU got 100), preserving party dominance on paper while letting unprecedented political pluralism into the chamber. The Congress then elected a standing Supreme Soviet from its ranks.
sources: Archie Brown, The Rise and Fall of Communism

questions.prompt: In March 1990 the Soviet Congress of People's Deputies amended the USSR Constitution to repeal which specific provision, ending the Communist Party's formal monopoly on political power?

choices.text: Article 6, which had declared the CPSU 'the leading and guiding force of Soviet society and the nucleus of its political system'
Article 70, which defined the USSR as a federation of socialist republics based on the principle of democratic centralism
choices.note: Article 70 of the 1977 Constitution addressed federal structure, not party monopoly; it was not the article repealed to open up multiparty politics.

choices.text: Article 39, which subordinated all civic associations to the oversight of the Central Committee
choices.note: Article 39 dealt with citizens' rights generally; the party's leading role was anchored in Article 6.

choices.text: Article 1, which proclaimed the USSR a 'socialist state of the whole people' under party guidance
choices.note: Article 1 was a general declaration and remained in force; the specific monopoly clause was Article 6.

questions.explanation: Article 6 of the 1977 ('Brezhnev') Constitution codified the CPSU's leading role. Its March 1990 repeal legalized multiparty politics in the USSR and is a canonical marker of the regime's political liberalization — formally ending what political scientists call the 'party-state' and setting up the short-lived Soviet multiparty period that preceded the August 1991 coup.
sources: Archie Brown, The Rise and Fall of Communism

questions.prompt: What was the 'parade of sovereignties' (1988-1991) in the late Soviet period?

choices.text: A wave of declarations by union republics and some autonomous regions asserting the supremacy of their own laws over USSR laws, beginning with the Baltics and culminating in Russia's own sovereignty declaration of June 1990
A series of military parades in Red Square at which each republic's leader symbolically handed back territorial powers to Moscow
choices.note: The name is metaphorical — it refers to legal sovereignty declarations, not actual parades, and the direction of transfer was from Moscow to the republics.

choices.text: A coordinated campaign by Gorbachev to grant the republics ceremonial sovereignty while retaining full central control under the Union Treaty
choices.note: The declarations were initiated by the republics themselves and substantively challenged central authority; Gorbachev did not orchestrate them, and the new Union Treaty he pushed was precisely an attempt to recontain them.

choices.text: A set of UN recognition proceedings in 1990-91 that individually admitted each Soviet republic as a sovereign state before the USSR formally dissolved
choices.note: UN admission of the post-Soviet states came after December 1991; the 'parade of sovereignties' was an internal-law phenomenon about legal supremacy inside the union, not international recognition.

questions.explanation: The parade of sovereignties names the cascading wave of republic-level declarations that their own laws superseded all-union laws, hollowing out the USSR from within. Estonia opened the wave in November 1988; Russia's declaration of sovereignty on 12 June 1990 (under Yeltsin, as chair of the RSFSR Supreme Soviet) was the tipping point because Russia was the core republic. By mid-1991 virtually every republic had declared sovereignty, setting up the 'war of laws' that preceded the August coup and the union's December 1991 dissolution.
sources: Archie Brown, The Rise and Fall of Communism

questions.prompt: On 1 December 1991 Ukrainian voters went to the polls in a nationwide referendum. What did they vote on, and what was the result?

choices.text: Whether to confirm Ukraine's 24 August 1991 declaration of independence — roughly 92 percent voted yes, with majorities in every region including Crimea
Whether to adopt a new Ukrainian constitution separating powers between a president and the Verkhovna Rada — the draft was rejected, delaying constitutional reform until 1996
choices.note: Ukraine's post-Soviet constitution was adopted by parliamentary vote in June 1996, not by referendum in December 1991.

choices.text: Whether Ukraine should enter a renewed Union Treaty with Russia and the other Soviet republics — the proposal was narrowly approved but overtaken by events
choices.note: The all-union referendum on preserving the USSR was held in March 1991, and Ukraine's December 1 vote moved in the opposite direction: confirming independence.

choices.text: Whether to ratify the Belovezha Accords dissolving the USSR — voters approved ratification alongside the election of Leonid Kravchuk as president
choices.note: The Belovezha Accords were signed on 8 December 1991 and ratified by the Verkhovna Rada, not by the December 1 referendum (which preceded them).

questions.explanation: The 1 December 1991 referendum was decisive for the USSR's dissolution: every Ukrainian region — including Crimea (where about 54 percent voted yes) and Sevastopol — backed independence, and Leonid Kravchuk was elected president the same day. Without Ukraine, Gorbachev's proposed new Union Treaty was unviable, and the Belovezha Accords formalizing the USSR's dissolution followed one week later.

questions.prompt: The August 1991 coup attempt against Gorbachev was launched by a self-declared 'State Committee for the State of Emergency' (GKChP). What was the immediate trigger for its timing?

choices.text: The plotters moved to pre-empt the scheduled 20 August 1991 signing of a new Union Treaty that would have devolved substantial powers from Moscow to the republics
Gorbachev had announced that he would resign as General Secretary of the CPSU the following week, and the plotters wanted to prevent a Yeltsin succession
choices.note: Gorbachev did not resign as General Secretary until after the coup (on 24 August 1991). The plotters' stated objective was preventing the Union Treaty, not forestalling a resignation.

choices.text: NATO had just extended invitations to the Baltic states to join the alliance, and hardliners wanted to intervene militarily before the invitations took effect
choices.note: NATO made no such invitations in 1991 — the Baltic states did not join NATO until 2004, and enlargement was not yet on the agenda. This is anachronistic.

choices.text: Yeltsin had introduced a bill in the Russian Supreme Soviet to formally withdraw the RSFSR from the USSR, and the plotters acted to block its passage
choices.note: The RSFSR did not pursue unilateral withdrawal in August 1991; the immediate trigger was the impending Union Treaty signing, not a Russian withdrawal bill.

questions.explanation: The new Union Treaty, negotiated during the Novo-Ogarevo process, was set to be signed on 20 August. It would have sharply weakened the central Soviet state, and conservative elements (defense, KGB, interior ministry, vice-president Yanaev) launched the coup on 19 August to prevent it. The coup collapsed within three days; it accelerated the very outcome it was meant to forestall, discrediting the central institutions and clearing the way for the December dissolution.
sources: Archie Brown, The Rise and Fall of Communism

questions.prompt: The Interregional Deputies' Group, formed in late July 1989 by deputies of the newly elected Congress of People's Deputies, played what role in late-Soviet political life?

choices.text: It was the first organized, quasi-opposition caucus within the Soviet legislative system — chaired jointly by Andrei Sakharov, Boris Yeltsin, Gavriil Popov, Yuri Afanasyev, and Viktor Palm — pushing for faster democratization, a market economy, and the repeal of Article 6
It was a Gorbachev-allied caucus created to whip Politburo loyalists in the new Congress and marginalize reformers like Yeltsin
choices.note: The Interregional Deputies Group was the main organized opposition to the Gorbachev-allied center-conservative majority, not a Politburo whipping operation. Yeltsin and Sakharov were among its co-chairs, not its targets.

choices.text: It was an inter-republican body that drafted the 1990 Union Treaty on behalf of the republic leaders
choices.note: The 1990-91 Union Treaty drafting happened in the separate Novo-Ogarevo process among republic leaders and Gorbachev, not in the Interregional Deputies Group.

choices.text: It was the Communist Party's own parliamentary caucus, functioning as the CPSU's legislative arm inside the Congress
choices.note: The CPSU's own legislative presence was organized through party discipline and did not require the Interregional Group. The IRG was explicitly the opposition caucus.

questions.explanation: The Interregional Deputies Group is the canonical late-Soviet opposition caucus — the first legal anti-regime parliamentary bloc in Soviet history, chaired by five co-leaders (including Sakharov, whose death in December 1989 shook the group). Its program anticipated the repeal of Article 6 (won in March 1990), radical market reform, and genuine republic-level sovereignty. Many of its members populated the post-1991 Russian political landscape — Yeltsin as president, Popov as Moscow mayor, Afanasyev as an intellectual fixture.
sources: Archie Brown, The Rise and Fall of Communism

questions.prompt: The 19th Conference of the CPSU in June-July 1988 is considered a pivot point in perestroika because:

choices.text: Gorbachev used it to push through a sweeping program of political reform — creating the Congress of People's Deputies, introducing contested elections, and shifting real authority from party organs toward newly empowered soviets
It formally dissolved the Communist Party's Politburo and replaced it with a presidential cabinet, merging state and party leadership under Gorbachev
choices.note: The Politburo was not dissolved in 1988; Gorbachev remained General Secretary with the Politburo intact. The presidential office was created later, in March 1990.

choices.text: It ratified the Brezhnev-era 'developed socialism' doctrine as the permanent ideological line of the USSR, blocking the market-oriented reforms Gorbachev had proposed
choices.note: The 19th Conference moved in the opposite direction — away from Brezhnev-era doctrine — and opened space for political, not closed-off, reform.

choices.text: It authorized the creation of a multiparty system and legalized the registration of non-communist parties for the first time since 1918
choices.note: Legal multipartism required repealing Article 6, which did not happen until March 1990. The 19th Conference did not itself authorize opposition parties.

questions.explanation: The 19th Party Conference was where Gorbachev laid out the plan for a new legislative architecture: a Congress of People's Deputies elected with contested races and a standing Supreme Soviet drawn from it. This was the first time a major political reform was publicly debated at a national party forum with live media coverage (a glasnost innovation), and the resulting constitutional amendments in late 1988 produced the legislature that would convene in 1989.
sources: Archie Brown, The Rise and Fall of Communism

questions.prompt: The October 1993 constitutional crisis in Russia ended when:

choices.text: Yeltsin ordered tanks to shell the White House (the Russian parliament building), dispersing the hardline-dominated Supreme Soviet led by Rutskoi and Khasbulatov
The Constitutional Court brokered a power-sharing deal between Yeltsin and the Supreme Soviet, preserving the 1978 constitution through 1996
choices.note: The crisis ended with force, not negotiation, and the 1978 RSFSR constitution was replaced in December 1993, shortly after the shelling.

choices.text: Yeltsin resigned the presidency in favor of Prime Minister Chernomyrdin, who then called new elections under an interim charter
choices.note: Yeltsin did not resign in 1993; he prevailed and pushed through a new constitution in December that concentrated power in the presidency.

choices.text: The Duma impeached Yeltsin and installed Aleksandr Lebed as acting president pending new elections
choices.note: There was no successful impeachment and no Duma existed in this form — the Duma was created by the new December 1993 constitution. Lebed did not hold the acting presidency at any point.

questions.explanation: The fight between Yeltsin and the Supreme Soviet (inherited from the 1978 RSFSR constitution) was a classic super-presidential-vs-super-parliamentary institutional clash. Yeltsin dissolved the parliament by decree in September; the parliament voted to remove him; the stand-off ended on 3-4 October 1993 when troops shelled the White House. The new December 1993 constitution written under Yeltsin's auspices and ratified by referendum locked in strong presidential powers — a legacy that shaped Russian politics through the Putin era.

questions.prompt: The Russian Constitution adopted by referendum in December 1993 is often described as a 'super-presidential' constitution. Which feature most clearly justifies that label?

choices.text: The president can dissolve the Duma if it rejects a prime minister three times or passes two no-confidence votes within three months, while the president's own impeachment requires a supermajority and Constitutional Court sign-off that makes it effectively impossible
The president serves an unlimited number of consecutive terms, subject only to a ceremonial vote of the Federation Council at each renewal
choices.note: The 1993 constitution originally capped consecutive terms at two (four years each, later six); the 2020 and 2008 amendments changed term length and nullified past terms, but the original document did not permit unlimited consecutive terms.

choices.text: The president personally appoints all 89 regional governors, bypassing any regional election
choices.note: Regional governors were popularly elected for most of the 1990s. Centrally appointed governors came in under Putin in 2004 and were partially rolled back later.

choices.text: The president can rewrite the constitution by decree without referendum or parliamentary involvement
choices.note: Constitutional amendment requires supermajorities in the Federal Assembly (and in some chapters also regional ratification or a Constitutional Assembly). Decree-level rewriting is not permitted.

questions.explanation: Scholars call the 1993 charter super-presidential because the president holds overwhelming formal powers — naming the prime minister, dissolving the Duma under several conditions, issuing binding decrees, and being largely insulated from impeachment — while the legislature has few compensating tools. Shugart-Carey-style presidential-power indices place Russia at the high end among presidential systems. This institutional design is one of the structural legacies of the October 1993 showdown with the Supreme Soviet.

questions.prompt: Vladimir Zhirinovsky's Liberal Democratic Party of Russia (LDPR) startled observers when it came first in the party-list voting of the December 1993 Duma elections. The LDPR is best characterized as:

choices.text: A far-right, ultranationalist party — its name notwithstanding — whose appeal rested on populist grievances and imperial nostalgia, not on classical liberalism
A centrist liberal-democratic party in the Western European mold, committed to market reform and EU integration
choices.note: Zhirinovsky built a reputation on bombastic nationalism, anti-Western rhetoric, and calls for reviving imperial borders — the opposite of Western European liberal-democratic programs.

choices.text: A social-democratic successor to the CPSU, emphasizing workers' rights and gradual market reform
choices.note: The CPSU's social-democratic successor space was occupied by Zyuganov's KPRF, not the LDPR. Zhirinovsky's platform was nationalist rather than socialist.

choices.text: A regional Cossack movement with concentrated support in southern Russia and little presence elsewhere
choices.note: The LDPR drew support across Russia, particularly among protest-minded and male voters in provincial Russia — not a regionally confined Cossack movement.

questions.explanation: The LDPR's 1993 result (about 22.9 percent of the party-list vote) was a shock because it revealed the electoral strength of populist-nationalist protest voting against shock therapy and the Yeltsin reform project. The party name is deliberately misleading; Zhirinovsky campaigned on restoring Russian imperial power, hostility to the 'near abroad' states, and anti-Western rhetoric. The result pushed the Yeltsin government toward slower economic reform and signaled the electoral opening that Putin's more disciplined statism would later fill.

questions.prompt: In the 1996 Russian presidential election Boris Yeltsin came from single-digit approval earlier in the year to defeat Communist Party leader Gennady Zyuganov in a two-round runoff. Most analysts attribute the turnaround to which combination of factors?

choices.text: A massive oligarch-funded media campaign (the 'Davos Pact'), lavish off-budget spending, the co-opting of ex-rival Aleksandr Lebed after the first round, and Kremlin-friendly framing of the contest as a choice between reform and a return to communism
A surprise peace agreement with Chechen fighters concluded on election morning, which gave Yeltsin a decisive late surge among military and security voters
choices.note: The Khasavyurt ceasefire in Chechnya was negotiated by Lebed in August 1996, after the runoff. It did not swing the vote.

choices.text: A landslide endorsement by Western leaders at a pre-election G7 summit, which dominated Russian television coverage
choices.note: Western leaders preferred Yeltsin but the decisive factors were domestic: oligarch-backed media, state resources, and the Lebed deal, not G7 endorsements.

choices.text: Zyuganov's withdrawal from the second round in favor of Yeltsin under an all-parties coalition agreement
choices.note: Zyuganov contested and lost the runoff; there was no withdrawal or coalition agreement.

questions.explanation: The 1996 election is a paradigm case of 'electoral authoritarianism'-adjacent tactics deployed by a hybrid regime to preserve reformist incumbents. Yeltsin's approval in January was in the single digits; by July he had won with 54 percent in the runoff. The oligarchs who backed him (Berezovsky, Gusinsky, Khodorkovsky and others) in exchange for loans-for-shares privatization access produced a one-sided media environment; Lebed's third-place finish (14.5 percent) was converted into an endorsement when Yeltsin appointed him security-council chief between rounds. The campaign is often cited as the moment when oligarchic capture of politics became entrenched.

questions.prompt: On 31 December 1999 Boris Yeltsin resigned the Russian presidency in a surprise televised address. Under the 1993 Constitution, who became acting president, and what follow-on event did the resignation trigger?

choices.text: Prime Minister Vladimir Putin became acting president and an early presidential election was triggered, which Putin won in the first round in March 2000
Federation Council Chair Yegor Stroyev became acting president for 90 days, and the scheduled June 2000 election proceeded as planned
choices.note: The 1993 Constitution places the prime minister, not the Federation Council chair, next in line. Putin, as PM since August 1999, became acting president.

choices.text: Duma Speaker Gennady Seleznyov became acting president pending a joint Federation Council / Duma vote to name a successor
choices.note: The acting presidency passes to the prime minister under Article 92, not to the Duma speaker or through a joint legislative vote.

choices.text: A constitutional vacancy required the Constitutional Court to schedule a referendum on Yeltsin's possible return, which failed in March 2000
choices.note: There was no such referendum. Putin took over as acting president under the ordinary succession rule and won the shortened election.

questions.explanation: Yeltsin's resignation elevated his hand-picked successor, Putin (PM since August 1999), to the acting presidency and moved the scheduled election forward from June to 26 March 2000. Putin won in the first round with about 53 percent, riding the wave of the Second Chechen War and the contrast with a frail, unpopular Yeltsin. The episode is a textbook case of managed succession inside a super-presidential constitution.

questions.prompt: In the July 1994 Ukrainian presidential election, Leonid Kuchma defeated the incumbent Leonid Kravchuk. What regional and programmatic cleavage did that contest expose?

choices.text: Kuchma drew disproportionately from the more Russian-speaking east and south and campaigned on closer ties with Russia and upgrading the status of the Russian language, while Kravchuk's base was in the center and west
Kuchma ran as the candidate of the far-right Ukrainian nationalist bloc, while Kravchuk represented the Communist Party of Ukraine
choices.note: The regional polarity ran the opposite way: Kuchma's support was concentrated in the Russian-speaking east/south, while Kravchuk, an ex-Communist ideology secretary, had re-positioned himself around state-building and drew the Ukrainophone center-west. Neither ran as a KPU candidate.

choices.text: Kuchma campaigned on rapid NATO and EU accession, while Kravchuk favored neutrality and a CIS security pact
